meta.title: Captions, as a Sidecar
meta.subject: How a feature plugin writes an SRT file alongside the rendered mp4

scenes.title: Captions, as a Sidecar
scenes.tagline: One plugin, one hook — an SRT file next to every mp4

beats.narration: Open the panel. This film is rendered the way every docent film is rendered — but a feature plugin watches the render finish and writes an SRT subtitle file next to the mp4.
The point is the protocol. A feature plugin can ship side-effects through the same hooks the engine already exposes — no fork, no monkeypatch.

scenes.heading: afterRender is the sidecar hook
scenes.statement: Every feature plugin can register an afterRender — the protocol's slot for sidecar writers like captions, transcripts, chapter markers.

beats.narration: The headline. The kit exposes one hook, afterRender, called by the orchestrator the moment the render stage returns. The hook receives the per-beat TTS timings and the spec's narration.
An SRT writer needs exactly those two inputs — when each beat played, what each beat said. The feature pack consumes them and writes the file. That is the entire integration.

scenes.heading: A feature plugin is one hook and one file
points: afterRender fires once the mp4 lands on disk.
It receives per-beat seconds + narration text in one record.
The plugin builds the SRT and writes it next to the video. Done.

beats.narration: The takeaway. The afterRender hook fires once the mp4 lands. The plugin receives per-beat timings and narration text. It writes the SRT next to the video.
Look in the output directory. The captions-demo.srt next to captions-demo.mp4 is the proof.
